But the iPhone, I can't begin to understand how my iPhone works, nor do I want to, nor would I ever explain how it works in order to sell the product. But if you're an engineer or you're a person who's invented something or you're founding a company that's created something, so often the people I work with, the business people, the engineers, the inventors, the innovators, the entrepreneurs, they always want to talk about how it works and sort of the inner story behind where it came from. And if that story actually is a personal story, then it's great. Like if Natalie built the robot lawn mower because of the situation that I just described, that would be great. But that's never the case. It's always someone saw a market opportunity that had not been exploited yet. It is very rare that someone like invents a thing because they desperately wanted it first because it was a pain point. When that happens, that's glorious and fantastic. it. ## [27:26 - 30:31]

was playing golf. I know which stories resonated with them. Oftentimes the way you can tell whether something means something to people is they ask you follow up questions. And if you tell them something and you get nothing back in terms of a follow up question, that story or that element of you or that part of you that you shared probably didn't resonate either because you didn't tell it right or it's just the wrong thing to be talking about. So a lot of it is sort of monitoring how the world is responding to you, responding to your stories, responding to the way you present yourself and figuring out if that response is the kind of response that you're looking for, if that's going to be a useful response to you. ## [1:00:57 - 1:04:02]

and her dog are stuck in the house while she's doing circles on grass, killing, you know, shortening grass so that they can play on it. And I don't want that for her anymore. You know, I would do it something as simple as that, but note that I'm trying to create as much mental imagery in their mind as possible so that when the elevator doors open and they walk away, Natalie mowing the lawn, three boys inside, a dog inside, all of that is an image that they're carrying with them rather than words. The words are forgotten, sentences are forgotten, images are remembered forever. cast in the negative light. So I think their brains automatically go, well, that's not me, because that was at a bowling alley in April. And that guy's name was John. And my name is Dave, right?
